narrative:
As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your solid LSAT score of 42—higher than the typical 36.7 average—and your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which is close to the standard 3.21. You're planning to attend full-time, just like over 90% of students in the dataset we used. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 60.6% probability that you would fail it. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed on the bar and graduate strong.

The model's decision hinged most on your Year 3 school grades, where you ranked in the 2nd decile—much lower than the average 5th or 6th decile for others. This was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as weaker performance in later undergrad years often signals challenges ahead on high-stakes exams like the bar. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, thanks to it being above average and showing your test-taking strength. Rounding out the top factors, your Year 1 grades in the 2nd decile—again below the typical 5th or 6th—nudged things further toward a failure risk, highlighting a pattern of early underperformance compared to peers.

For students like you, who share similar demographic backgrounds, the model sees these lower grade deciles as red flags because they deviate sharply from the stronger academic trajectories of those who typically pass the bar first time. Your LSAT helps balance that, but not enough to offset the grades in the eyes of the prediction. To turn this around, focus on boosting your academic deciles—perhaps through targeted prep courses, additional undergrad credits, or retaking key classes to climb into the top half. Consider retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and gather letters emphasizing your resilience as a Black man from an upper-middle background pursuing law. Many applicants in tough spots reapply after addressing these gaps, so use this feedback to build a stronger profile for the next cycle.
